skill.name: jadx
skill.description: Guides authorized Android APK decompilation with jadx, including source extraction, search workflows, and security review checklists.
skill.summary: Decompile Android APKs with jadx and inspect Java source for security findings.
skill.author: BrownFineSecurity

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer flagged many shell, secret, crypto, and reconnaissance terms, but review shows they are examples in a jadx usage guide. The confirmed risk is legitimate but sensitive external command guidance for decompiling APKs and searching extracted source, which requires authorization and careful path handling.

medium_findings.title: External Command Guidance for APK Analysis
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs users to run jadx, grep, tee, and shell loops against APK files and generated output. This is expected for a jadx skill, but it can execute local commands and write files, so users must validate paths and only analyze authorized APKs.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are explicit and numerous, but they are documentation for a security tool rather than hidden execution code. Risk depends on whether an agent runs them with untrusted filenames or unauthorized APKs.

low_findings.title: Secret and Environment Findings Are Search Patterns
low_findings.description: The API key and related static matches appear inside grep patterns used to find secrets in decompiled Android applications. No evidence found that the skill reads local environment variables or exfiltrates secrets.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged tokens are quoted search terms inside shell examples. The surrounding text explains that users should search decompiled app source for hardcoded credentials.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto Findings Are Audit Keywords
low_findings.description: The DES, MD5, and SHA1 matches are used as examples of insecure cryptography to search for during Android review. No evidence found that the skill implements or recommends weak cryptography.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The weak algorithm names appear in a vulnerability checklist and grep examples. The semantic context is detection of risky app code, not use of those algorithms by the skill.

low_findings.title: Network Scanning Reference Is Dual-Use Context
low_findings.description: The skill mentions using nmap and other IoT tools after extracting device communication details. This is dual-use security guidance, but the skill also states that APK analysis should be authorized.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network tool reference is present and security-sensitive, but it is not an embedded scanner or malicious automation. The ethics section limits use to authorized testing.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Commands and Pipelines in Documentation
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill contains many bash examples for decompilation, recursive searches, output redirection, and batch processing. These commands are useful but should be reviewed before execution in an AI-assisted environment.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The dangerous pattern is confirmed as documented shell usage. It is not malicious by itself, but it can affect local files and should not be executed blindly.

content.value_statement: Android APK review is slow when bytecode and resources are hard to inspect. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through jadx workflows for readable source analysis.
actual_capabilities: Explains basic and advanced jadx command-line decompilation options.
Shows how to decompile APKs with deobfuscation, fallback mode, and resource controls.
Provides grep-based workflows for finding secrets, URLs, crypto code, and authentication logic.
Compares jadx with apktool for code, resources, and smali review.
Outlines GUI workflows for browsing classes, search results, and cross-references.
Includes security and ethics guidance for authorized APK analysis.
limitations: Requires jadx, Java, and enough disk space to be installed locally.
Decompiled Java is approximate and may not match original source exactly.
Heavily obfuscated or protected APKs may produce incomplete output.
Does not verify findings without runtime analysis or manual review.

use_cases.title: Review a Mobile App for Secrets
use_cases.description: Decompile an authorized APK and search extracted source for hardcoded credentials, tokens, URLs, and storage risks.

use_cases.title: Understand Android App Behavior
use_cases.description: Convert DEX bytecode into readable Java and trace activities, services, APIs, and authentication flows.

use_cases.title: Analyze IoT Companion Apps
use_cases.description: Extract device endpoints, discovery logic, and protocol clues from an IoT mobile application.

prompt_templates.title: Basic APK Decompile
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the jadx skill to decompile this authorized APK. Explain the output folders and list the first files I should inspect.

prompt_templates.title: Search for Sensitive Data
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the jadx workflow to search the decompiled source for API keys, credentials, tokens, URLs, and database connection strings.

prompt_templates.title: Trace Authentication Logic
prompt_templates.prompt: Guide me through finding login, authorization, JWT, bearer token, and API client code in the jadx output.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Security Review Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a jadx-based review plan for this obfuscated APK, including deobfuscation, crypto checks, WebView checks, storage checks, and runtime validation steps.

output_examples.input: Decompile app.apk and tell me where to begin.
output_examples.output: The skill would recommend decompilation with deobfuscation, then review sources, resources, AndroidManifest.xml, and likely entry-point classes.

output_examples.input: Find security issues in the decompiled app.
output: Crypto, storage, WebView, and manifest review checklist

output_examples.input: The output is obfuscated and some methods failed.
output_examples.output: The skill would suggest deobfuscation options, fallback mode, showing bad code, and comparing results with apktool or runtime analysis.

best_practices: Only decompile APKs that you own or have explicit permission to assess.
Use deobfuscation for production apps and keep the original APK unchanged.
Confirm static findings with manual review and runtime testing before reporting.
anti_patterns: Do not run generated commands without checking file paths and output directories.
Do not treat decompiled Java as exact original source code.
Do not distribute proprietary decompiled source without authorization.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps use jadx to decompile Android APKs and inspect readable Java source for security and behavior analysis.

faq.question: Does it install jadx?
faq.answer: No. It assumes jadx and Java are already installed on the local system.

faq.question: Can it analyze obfuscated apps?
faq.answer: Yes, it explains jadx deobfuscation options, but heavily protected apps may still be difficult to read.

faq.question: Is this safe for marketplace use?
faq.answer: It is publishable with caution because it documents shell commands and dual-use security workflows.

faq.question: Does it replace runtime testing?
faq.answer: No. Static jadx findings should be confirmed with runtime analysis and manual validation.

faq.question: What tools does it complement?
faq.answer: It complements apktool, jadx-gui, grep, mobile proxies, and runtime instrumentation tools used in authorized assessments.
